steps.title: Identify what the hypothesis predicts
steps.content: The second research team hypothesizes that greater pressure fluctuations lead to better obstacle avoidance in low visibility. From Yanagisuru's findings, narrow-headed fish experience greater pressure fluctuations as vortex size increases, while wide-headed fish experience low pressure fluctuations. Therefore, the hypothesis predicts that narrow-headed fish should avoid obstructions better (fewer collisions) and wide-headed fish should avoid them worse (more collisions).

steps.title: Find the choice that shows the predicted pattern
steps.content:
Choice C directly demonstrates this: wide-headed catfish bumped into obstructions more often than narrow-headed needlefish. Since wide-headed fish feel weaker pressure fluctuations (from the first study), they should avoid obstacles less effectively—which is exactly what C shows. Choices A and B involve only narrow-headed fish or show equal collision rates between the two groups, neither of which tests the hypothesis. Choice D shows only that wide-headed fish bumped into more than half the obstructions, but lacks the comparison to narrow-headed fish needed to confirm the hypothesis.

That matches choice C — C is correct.
